And from the encoder you calculate embeddings and make predictions.
And so you have a small set of like very high quality data.
And this small set of very high quality data you can basically trust that all of the data is not noisy.
Like maybe it's manually annotated data and you have like 5,000 examples of it or something like that.
And then separately from that you have like 5 million examples of automatically labeled data that might be good or might not be good.
And so what they do is essentially at the beginning they take this encoder get embeddings make predictions over the high quality data.
And then they have a separate noise modeling layer where what this noise modeling layer does is it has a transition matrix, which says given that this.

And so that means that you can basically smooth out this distribution and account for the fact that labels may be noisy or may may not be noisy.

Then they add additional normalization on this transition matrix using something called trace normalization to move this matrix closer to the identity function, which says that the predictions are probably not wrong all the time.
The predictions are probably correct, you know a lot of the time they're not correct all the time.
So then you have that trace normalization competing with this trying to give you like a more smooth distribution and reduce your wall like reduce your loss.
